Scenario_Name: TFA Triggered for Every Login - Configuration
Given: System is configured for 'TFA every login' mode
When: A registered user attempts to login with valid credentials
Then: TFA is prompted immediately for every login attempt without exception

Given: TFA frequency is set to 'every login'
When: User logs in, logs out, and attempts to login again within the same day
Then: TFA is required again immediately for the second login attempt

Given: System is configured for 'TFA daily' mode
When: A registered user completes their first login of the day with TFA
Then: On subsequent logins the same day, TFA should not be required

Scenario_Name: Multiple Logins in Daily Mode - Same Day
Given: TFA frequency is set to 'daily' and user logged in once that day
When: The same user logs out and logs in again later the same day
Then: TFA should not be triggered for the second login on the same day

Scenario_Name: TFA Reset at Midnight - Daily Mode
Given: TFA frequency is set to 'daily' and user has already logged in today
When: The system clock advances to the next calendar day at midnight
Then: On the next day, TFA should be required again for login

Scenario_Name: Configuration Change by Admin - Every to Daily
Given: System is configured to 'TFA every login'; admin changes it to 'daily'
When: Admin applies the configuration change
Then: Existing sessions are unaffected; new logins follow the 'daily' frequency rule

Scenario_Name: Configuration Change by Admin - Daily to Every
Given: System is configured to 'TFA daily'; admin changes it to 'every login'
When: Admin applies the configuration change
Then: Active users on next login will be prompted for TFA per the new 'every login' rule

Given: Admin changes TFA frequency configuration
When: The configuration change is applied
Then: The system logs the change with timestamp, admin user ID, previous setting, and new setting

Scenario_Name: Configuration Takes Effect Immediately for New Sessions
Given: TFA frequency is changed from 'daily' to 'every login'
When: Existing sessions remain active; new login attempt is made
Then: New sessions respect the new frequency setting; existing sessions are not retroactively affected

Given: Two admins attempt to change TFA frequency configuration simultaneously
When: Both configuration change requests reach the system at the same time
Then: System handles gracefully: last-write-wins or provides a locking/conflict resolution mechanism

Given: Admin attempts to set TFA frequency to an unsupported value (e.g., 'weekly')
When: Admin submits the invalid configuration
Then: System rejects the change and displays error; valid options are clearly presented

Given: Multiple users are actively logged in
When: Administrator changes TFA frequency configuration
Then: Active sessions continue under existing rules; next login attempts apply new frequency rules

Given: TFA frequency is set to 'daily' with users in multiple timezones
When: Users in different timezones login around midnight local time
Then: Daily TFA reset is applied correctly based on user's local timezone, not server timezone

Given: TFA frequency is set to 'daily' and DST transition occurs
When: Users login during DST transition time
Then: System handles DST change correctly without duplicating or skipping TFA prompts

Given: A new system installation is deployed
When: System initializes without explicit configuration
Then: Default TFA frequency is set to 'every login' (recommended secure default)

Given: TFA frequency is set to a specific value
When: System is restarted (power cycle
Then: deployment)
Related_AC: Configuration persists; TFA frequency remains as previously configured

Given: Admin changes TFA frequency configuration and later wants to revert
When: Admin initiates a rollback to previous configuration
Then: Previous configuration is restored; users' next login attempts follow the restored frequency rule

Given: An administrator accesses system settings
When: Admin navigates to TFA configuration section
Then: Current TFA frequency setting is clearly displayed with options to change it

Given: Admin needs to audit TFA frequency history
When: Admin generates a report of configuration changes
Then: The system provides a detailed log showing all frequency changes with timestamps and who made them

Given: Admin changes TFA frequency configuration multiple times rapidly
When: Rapid configuration changes are applied
Then: System processes configuration changes efficiently without performance degradation or race conditions
